title: Quiz: Unit Testing Quantum Programs
description: Test your understanding of quantum unit testing strategies.

questions.question: Why is quantum testing more challenging than classical testing?

options.text: Outputs are probabilistic and measurement collapses state

questions.explanation: Quantum outputs are probabilistic, and measurement destroys the quantum state, making direct state comparison impossible without simulation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a mock quantum backend?

options.text: To test code in isolation without real quantum execution

questions.explanation: Mock backends allow testing the classical control logic without actually executing quantum circuits, enabling isolation testing.

questions.question: When validating a Bell state |00> + |11>, what outcomes should have approximately 50% probability each?
questions.explanation: The Bell state (|00> + |11>)/sqrt(2) produces only correlated outcomes: both qubits measure 0 or both measure 1.

questions.question: Statevector simulation allows exact verification of quantum states without measurement.
questions.explanation: Statevector simulators compute the full quantum state, allowing exact comparison without the randomness of measurement.

questions.question: What statistical test validates that measured outcomes match expected probability distribution?
questions.explanation: Chi-squared goodness-of-fit test compares observed frequency distribution against expected probabilities.

questions.question: Why might you increase the number of shots in a quantum test?

options.text: To make the circuit run faster
To reduce statistical uncertainty in probability estimates

questions.explanation: More shots reduce statistical noise, giving more accurate probability estimates and tighter confidence intervals.

questions.question: What does 'tolerance' represent in a quantum test case?

options.text: Maximum allowed deviation from expected probability

questions.explanation: Tolerance defines how far measured probabilities can deviate from expected values while still passing the test.
